inference_unit.problem: The paper addresses the lack of an explicit algebraic model, built directly from differential forms and Chen's iterated integrals, that realizes the Chas–Sullivan loop product on the homology of the free loop space. Concretely, for a compact oriented manifold $M$ one has the topological construction of an associative, graded-commutative "loop product" on the shifted homology $\mathbb{H}_*(LM)=H_{*+d}(LM)$ (Chas–Sullivan), and independent algebraic descriptions of loop-space cohomology via Chen's iterated integrals (identifying $H^*(LM)$ with the cyclic bar complex cohomology for simply-connected $M$) and of related algebraic models (e.g. Merkulov's model using formal power-series connections). However, prior to this work there was no explicit, intrinsic construction using the cyclic bar complex or the dual bar complexes of de Rham forms that (a) produces a chain-level differential graded complex built from $A\subset\Lambda M$ (a dga of forms quasi-isomorphic to the de Rham algebra), (b) carries a concrete product (defined at the level of Hom-complexes) whose induced product on homology is identified with the geometric loop product, and (c) extends the iterated-integral perspective to non-simply-connected manifolds and to the algebraic incarnation of the Goldman bracket. Existing approaches left gaps: Merkulov's model uses formal completions and connections rather than the cyclic bar duals; Chen's iterated-integral isomorphisms identify cohomology groups but do not directly produce the algebra structure corresponding to the Chas–Sullivan product; and the relation between augmentation-completions of group rings (or conjugacy-class completions) and dual bar complexes was not fully developed in this iterated-integral framework. These gaps prevent a direct algebraic computation or conceptual understanding of the loop product and the Goldman bracket purely in terms of iterated integrals and normalized Hom-complexes of differential forms, and they obstruct extensions to non-simply-connected manifolds without additional hypotheses.

conclusion.title: Definition of Hom(B(A),A) and its normalized subcomplex with cup product

conclusions.conclusion:
A differential graded complex $\operatorname{Hom}(B(A),A)$ is defined for any differential graded algebra $A$, where $B(A)=\bigoplus_{r\ge0}\otimes^r sA$ is the bar complex with differential $d_B$ (given on generators $(\omega_1,\dots,\omega_r)$ by the sum of terms inserting $d$ on each factor and terms multiplying adjacent factors as in the displayed $d_B$ formula). The graded pieces are $$ \operatorname{Hom}(B(A),A)_n=\bigoplus_{p-q=n}\operatorname{Hom}(B(A)_p,A^q), $$ and the differential $\delta$ on $\operatorname{Hom}(B(A),A)$ is given for $\varphi\in\operatorname{Hom}(B(A),A)$ by the explicit formula $$ \begin{aligned} \delta\varphi(\omega_1,\ldots,\omega_r)&=(-1)^{|\varphi|-\varepsilon_r}d\varphi(\omega_1,\ldots,\omega_r)-(-1)^{|\varphi|-\varepsilon_r}\varphi(d_B(\omega_1,\ldots,\omega_r))\\ &\quad+(-1)^{|\varphi|-\varepsilon_r}\omega_1\wedge\varphi(\omega_2,\ldots,\omega_r)\\ &\quad-(-1)^{(|\omega_r|+1)(|\varphi|+1)}\varphi(\omega_1,\ldots,\omega_{r-1})\wedge\omega_r, \end{aligned} $$ where $\varepsilon_r=\deg(\omega_1,\dots,\omega_r)$. A normalized subcomplex $\overline{\operatorname{Hom}}(B(A),A)\subset\operatorname{Hom}(B(A),A)$ is specified by Chen-style cyclic normalization equations: for all $\omega,\omega_i\in A^{>0}$ and $f\in A^0$ the three displayed relations (involving insertion of $f$ and $df$ in adjacent positions and left/right wedge actions) must hold. On $\operatorname{Hom}(B(A),A)$ a cup product $\cup$ is defined by $$ \varphi_1\cup\varphi_2(\omega_1,\ldots,\omega_r)=\sum_{0\le i\le r}(-1)^{|\varphi_1|(|\varphi_2|+\varepsilon_r-\varepsilon_i)}\varphi_1(\omega_1,\ldots,\omega_i)\wedge\varphi_2(\omega_{i+1},\ldots,\omega_r), $$ which is a chain-level product satisfying the Leibniz rule $\delta(\varphi_1\cup\varphi_2)=\delta\varphi_1\cup\varphi_2+(-1)^{|\varphi_1|}\varphi_1\cup\delta\varphi_2$, hence induces an associative product on $H_*(\operatorname{Hom}(B(A),A))$ that restricts to $H_*(\overline{\operatorname{Hom}}(B(A),A))$.
In loop-space string topology one needs an explicit algebraic complex, built from differential forms, whose homology carries a product corresponding to the Chas–Sullivan loop product. Prior constructions either identified loop-space (co)homology abstractly (Chen's iterated integrals identify $H^*(LM)$ with cyclic bar cohomology) or produced models using formal power series completions (Merkulov), but there was no explicit Hom-complex formed from the bar complex $B(A)$ and the algebra $A$ together with normalization conditions and an explicit cup product that is internal to differential forms, and that is amenable to spectral-sequence computations. The absence of such an explicit normalized Hom-complex prevented a transparent algebraic realization of loop homology directly from de Rham data and obstructed comparisons with classical algebraic structures (augmentation ideals, cyclic actions, Connes operator).

conclusion.title: Algebra isomorphism between loop homology and Hom-complex homology (Theorem 1.1)

conclusions.conclusion:
Let $M$ be a compact closed oriented simply-connected smooth manifold of dimension $d$ with $H_*(M)$ of finite type. Let $A\subset\Lambda M$ be a differential graded subalgebra with the inclusion inducing an isomorphism $H^*(A)\cong H^*(\Lambda M)$. Then there is an isomorphism of associative, graded-commutative algebras $$ \mathbb{H}_*(LM)\;\cong\; H_*(\overline{\operatorname{Hom}}(B(A),A)), $$ where $\mathbb{H}_*(LM)=H_{*+d}(LM)$ is the Chas–Sullivan loop homology (shifted by $d$) equipped with the loop product, and the product on the right is the cup product induced on $H_*(\overline{\operatorname{Hom}}(B(A),A))$. Under this isomorphism the product on $H_*(\overline{\operatorname{Hom}}(B(A),A))$ corresponds to the geometric loop product.
The principal conceptual gap addressed here is the absence of a direct and verifiable algebraic model, entirely in terms of a chosen de Rham-type dga $A$, whose homology reproduces not only the homology groups of the free loop space but also the Chas–Sullivan loop product. Prior to this statement one could either compute loop homology topologically, or use higher-level formalisms (e.g. Merkulov's formal power-series connection), or identify cohomology via Chen's iterated integrals without an explicit product realizer; but there was no self-contained construction showing that the normalized Hom-complex $ \overline{\operatorname{Hom}}(B(A),A)$, defined from $A$ and the bar complex $B(A)$ with Chen normalization, carries a cup product whose induced algebra on homology equals loop homology with its loop product. The result fills this gap by giving precise hypotheses (simply-connectedness, finite type homology, existence of $A$ quasi-isomorphic to de Rham forms) and by identifying the chain-level product that realizes the geometric loop product on homology.

conclusion.title: Poincaré-type chain map P: degree-shift isomorphism (Lemma 4.2)

conclusions.conclusion:
For $M$ a compact oriented $d$-manifold and $A\subset\Lambda M$ a dga as above, define a chain map $P:\overline{\operatorname{Hom}}(B(A),A)\to\overline{\operatorname{Hom}}(B(A),A^\vee)$ by $$ P(\varphi)(\omega_1,\ldots,\omega_n)(\omega)=\int_M \omega\wedge \varphi(\omega_1,\ldots,\omega_n), $$ where $A^\vee$ denotes the graded dual of $A$ and the right-hand side is integration of the wedge product over $M$. Then $P$ preserves the natural filtrations and induces an isomorphism on homology $$ H_*(\overline{\operatorname{Hom}}(B(A),A))\;\cong\; H_{*-d}(\overline{\operatorname{Hom}}(B(A),A^\vee)). $$
To relate the Hom-complex built from $A$ (with values in $A$) to the dual Hom-complex (with values in $A^\vee$) which is the natural target of Chen-type iterated-integral maps from singular chains in $LM$, one requires an explicit Poincaré-type pairing at the chain level. Without such an explicit $P$ and the verification that it is a filtered quasi-isomorphism (inducing the indicated degree shift on homology), one cannot compare $H_*(\overline{\operatorname{Hom}}(B(A),A))$ to the known iterated-integral models of $H_*(LM)$ or transport algebra structures across Poincaré duality. This is particularly crucial because Chen's identification of loop-space homology/cohomology produces objects valued in $A^\vee$, so a precise chain-level duality is needed to move between $A$-valued and $A^\vee$-valued Hom-complexes and to identify their algebra structures with geometric loop operations.

conclusion.title: Iterated-integral description of completed conjugacy-class algebra (Theorem 5.1)

conclusions.conclusion:
Let $M$ be a smooth manifold with $H_*(M)$ of finite type. Let $\pi=\pi_1(M)$, let $\tilde{\pi}$ be the set of conjugacy classes of $\pi$, let $J\subset\mathbb{R}\pi$ be the augmentation ideal and $\tilde{J}^p=\mathrm{pr}(J^p)\subset\mathbb{R}\tilde{\pi}$ its projection. Let $A\subset\Lambda M$ be a differential graded subalgebra with $A^0=\mathbb{R}$ such that the inclusion induces isomorphisms $H^q(A)\to H^q(\Lambda M)$ for $q=0,1$ and an injection for $q=2$. Then there is a natural isomorphism of vector spaces $$ \varprojlim_{p}\mathbb{R}\tilde{\pi}/\tilde{J}^p\;\cong\; H_0(\overline{\operatorname{Hom}}(B(A),A^\vee)), $$ where the right-hand side is the degree-zero homology of the normalized Hom-complex with values in $A^\vee$.
For non-simply-connected manifolds, the algebraic object naturally associated to iterated integrals is closely tied to the completion of the fundamental group (or its conjugacy classes) with respect to powers of the augmentation ideal. Chen proved analogous statements for completions of $\pi$ itself, but a clear identification between the completion of the conjugacy-class algebra $\varprojlim_p\mathbb{R}\tilde{\pi}/\tilde{J}^p$ and a Hom-complex built from differential forms (the $H_0$ of a normalized cyclic-dual Hom-complex) was not established in the iterated-integral framework under mild cohomological hypotheses. Without such an identification one cannot treat loop-space algebraic structures (which are sensitive to free homotopy classes) for non-simply-connected manifolds purely in terms of iterated integrals. Theorem 5.1 supplies the missing bridge under explicit cohomological hypotheses (degree 0,1 isomorphisms and degree 2 injectivity).

Let $\tilde{J}^p$ denote the projected power-$p$ augmentation ideal in $\mathbb{R}\tilde{\pi}$. Then the following commutator containment relations hold: (1) For $p\ge1$ and $q\ge2$, $$ [\tilde{J}^p,\tilde{J}^q]\subset \tilde{J}^{\,p+q-2}. $$ (2) For $p\ge2$, $$ [\tilde{J}^p,\mathbb{R}\tilde{\pi}]\subset \tilde{J}^{\,p-1}. $$ These containments show that the commutator bracket is compatible with the filtration and therefore induces a well-defined Lie algebra structure on the inverse limit $\varprojlim_p\mathbb{R}\tilde{\pi}/\tilde{J}^p$.
To equip the completed conjugacy-class algebra $\varprojlim_p\mathbb{R}\tilde{\pi}/\tilde{J}^p$ with a Lie algebra structure induced from the commutator of loops (Goldman bracket), one must check that commutators respect the augmentation-filtration so that brackets descend to the inverse limit. Prior to this verification it is unclear whether the naive commutator on representatives in $\mathbb{R}\tilde{\pi}$ induces a continuous Lie bracket on the completion. Proposition 6.2 supplies the precise filtration compatibilities (including the $-2$ and $-1$ shifts) needed to define the inverse-limit Lie algebra.

conclusion.title: Filtered degree-shift isomorphism used in bracket construction (Proposition 6.3)

conclusions.conclusion:
For the normalized filtered complexes, for each $p$ there is a chain-level Poincaré-type map $P$ (defined as in conclusion 3 by integration over $M$) inducing an isomorphism on homology $$ H_{*}\big(\overline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F^{p}B(A),A)\big)\;\cong\; H_{*-2}\big(\overline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F^{p}B(A),A^\vee)\big). $$ This degree-$2$ shift isomorphism preserves the $F^p$ filtration and is used to relate cup products in $A$-valued Hom-homology to corresponding operations in $A^\vee$-valued Hom-homology when constructing the Lie bracket.
The construction of a Lie bracket on the Hom-complex side that corresponds to the Goldman bracket requires transferring cup-product data between $A$-valued and $A^\vee$-valued Hom-complexes while controlling degrees and filtrations. A filtered isomorphism that shifts degree by $2$ on the $F^p$-pieces furnishes the necessary algebraic ingredient to compose Connes's operator with cup products and Poincaré duality in order to produce a bracket. Without this filtered degree-shift isomorphism one cannot form the bracket in a well-defined filtered way that matches the geometric intersection-derived commutator.

conclusion.title: Chain-level bracket on Hom-complex via Connes operator and cup product

conclusions.conclusion:
A chain-level bracket on $H_0(\overline{\operatorname{Hom}}(B(A),A^\vee))$ is constructed as follows. Let $B$ denote the Connes operator $$ B: H_*(\overline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F^{p}B(A),A^\vee))\to H_{*+1}(\overline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F^{p-1}B(A),A^\vee)) $$ defined on classes by the displayed cyclic sum $$ \begin{aligned} B(\varphi)(\omega_1,\ldots,\omega_{p-1})(\omega) &=\sum_{0\le k\le p-1}(-1)^{(\varepsilon_k+1)(\varepsilon_{p-1}-\varepsilon_k)}\\ &\quad\times\varphi(\omega_{k+1},\ldots,\omega_{p-1},\omega,\omega_1,\ldots,\omega_k)(1). \end{aligned} $$ Using the filtered isomorphism $P$ of conclusion 6, for $\varphi_1,\varphi_2\in H_0(\overline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F^pB(A),A^\vee))$ define $$ [\varphi_1,\varphi_2]\;=\;-P\big( P^{-1}B\varphi_1\;\cup\; P^{-1}B\varphi_2\big)\ \in\ H_0(\overline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F^{p-1}B(A),A^\vee)), $$ where $\cup$ is the cup product on $H_*(\overline{\operatorname{Hom}}(B(A),A))$. This operation yields a bilinear bracket descending along the filtration and is the algebraic counterpart of the Goldman/Chas–Sullivan commutator at the Hom-complex level.
To compare the Goldman bracket (an intersection-derived Lie bracket on free homotopy classes of loops) with an algebraic bracket on the Hom-complex side one needs an explicit recipe that uses standard algebraic operators available in the cyclic bar/Hom context: the Connes operator $B$, the cup product on Hom-homology, and Poincaré duality maps $P$ and $P^{-1}$. Prior to this construction there was no concrete formula exhibiting how these three operations combine to give a bracket on $H_0(\overline{\operatorname{Hom}}(B(A),A^\vee))$ that matches geometric intersection pairings. The displayed definition fills that procedural gap and gives a computable algebraic bracket.

conclusion.title: Compatibility diagram between augmentation-graded brackets and spectral-sequence brackets (Proposition 6.4)
Goldman bracket recovered as Lie algebra on Hom-complex and identification for surfaces (Theorem 6.1)

conclusions.conclusion:
Let $M$ be a compact closed oriented surface of genus $g$. Then the Goldman bracket induces a Lie algebra structure on the inverse limit $\varprojlim_p\mathbb{R}\tilde{\pi}/\tilde{J}^p$. Moreover there is an isomorphism of Lie algebras $$ \varprojlim_{p}\mathbb{R}\tilde{\pi}/\tilde{J}^{p}\;\cong\; H_{0}\big(\operatorname{Hom}(B(H^*(M)),H^*(M)^\vee)\big), $$ where on the right $H^*(M)$ is regarded as a differential graded algebra with trivial differential, the Lie bracket is the one obtained via the Connes operator, cup product and Poincaré maps as in conclusions 6 and 7, and the isomorphism uses formality (the $dd^c$ lemma) to replace $A$ by $H^*(M)$ in the simply-connected/surface case.
For closed oriented surfaces the classical Goldman bracket equips the vector space spanned by free homotopy classes of loops with a Lie algebra structure. Relating this geometric Lie algebra to an iterated-integral/Hom-complex algebra requires three steps: (i) identify the completed conjugacy algebra with $H_0$ of a normalized Hom-complex (Theorem 5.1), (ii) produce a Hom-complex bracket mirroring intersection, and (iii) pass to cohomology algebra generators in a way that preserves the bracket. For surfaces the formality (via the $dd^c$ lemma) permits replacing $A$ by $H^*(M)$, so that the algebraic bracket constructed from Connes operator and cup product on the Hom-complex side can be shown to coincide with the Goldman bracket. Before this result the precise Lie-algebra identification in the iterated-integral framework for surfaces was not established; this conclusion completes the identification under the surface/formality hypotheses.

inference_unit.open_question: The paper's results are obtained under explicit hypotheses and several natural generalizations are left unresolved within the text. The main acknowledged limitations and open directions are: (1) Theorem 1.1 assumes $M$ is simply-connected and $H_*(M)$ is of finite type; extending the algebra isomorphism $\mathbb{H}_*(LM)\cong H_*(\overline{\operatorname{Hom}}(B(A),A))$ to non-simply-connected manifolds without passing to degree-zero completions is not treated. (2) Theorem 5.1, identifying the completed conjugacy-class algebra $\varprojlim_p\mathbb{R}\tilde{\pi}/\tilde{J}^p$ with $H_0(\overline{\operatorname{Hom}}(B(A),A^\vee))$, is proved under the cohomological hypothesis that $H^q(A)\to H^q(\Lambda M)$ is an isomorphism for $q=0,1$ and injective for $q=2$; removing or weakening these cohomological injectivity/isomorphy conditions is not addressed. (3) The Lie-algebra identification of Theorem 6.1 for the Goldman bracket relies on formality (the $dd^c$ lemma) to replace $A$ by $H^*(M)$; extending the Lie-algebra isomorphism to surfaces or higher-dimensional manifolds that are not formal (or to understand obstructions when formality fails) is not resolved. (4) The paper constructs a filtered algebraic bracket and matches it on associated graded pieces to augmentation-graded brackets, but a full analysis of convergence/continuity issues or explicit computations in non-formal higher-dimensional examples is not pursued. These limitations are explicitly present in the hypotheses of the main theorems and indicate the primary directions for further work.
